name: APT34 IIS Backdoor DLL Deployment - CacheHttp Pattern
description: Detects creation of DLL files matching known APT34 IIS backdoor patterns (CacheHttp.dll, HttpProxy.dll, WebCache.dll) or DLL files written to IIS module directories. APT34 deploys passive IIS native modules that intercept HTTP requests for command delivery and data exfiltration.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
query: file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "dll" and (file.name : ("CacheHttp.dll", "HttpProxy.dll", "IISModule.dll", "WebCache.dll", "HttpCacher.dll") or file.path : ("*\\inetsrv\\*", "*\\IIS\\*", "*\\w3svc\\*", "*\\inetpub\\*")) and not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\msiexec.exe", "C:\\Windows\\servicing\\TrustedInstaller.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\TiWorker.exe")
note:
## Triage
- Verify the process creating the DLL is legitimate
- Check if the DLL exports IIS CHttpModule interface methods (RegisterModule, OnBeginRequest)
- Determine if IIS module registration was also attempted via appcmd.exe
- Correlate with IIS applicationHost.config modifications
- Check for HTTP interception patterns (X-Cache-Http header)

## False Positives
- Legitimate IIS module installations by administrators
- Software deployments that register IIS modules
- Microsoft IIS feature installation via Server Manager
false_positives: Legitimate IIS module installations by administrators
Software deployments that register IIS native modules
Microsoft IIS feature installation via Server Manager

name: IIS Native Module Registration via appcmd.exe LOLBin
description: Detects use of appcmd.exe to install IIS native modules. APT34 uses appcmd.exe as a living-off-the-land binary (LOLBin) to register CacheHttp.dll as a native IIS HTTP module, enabling passive HTTP request interception for C2 and data exfiltration.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
query: process where event.action == "start" and process.name : "appcmd.exe" and process.args : "install" and process.args : "module"
note:
## Triage
- Examine the full command line for the module name and image path
- Verify the DLL being registered is a legitimate Microsoft or vendor module
- Check if the parent process is part of a deployment pipeline or manual admin action
- Correlate with DLL file creation events in IIS directories
- Review IIS applicationHost.config for unauthorized module entries

## False Positives
- Legitimate IIS administration during maintenance windows
- Automated deployment pipelines that manage IIS modules
- Server provisioning scripts
false_positives: Legitimate IIS administration during maintenance windows
Automated deployment pipelines that manage IIS modules
Server provisioning scripts

name: LSA Password Filter DLL Registration - Credential Interception
description: Detects modification of the HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Lsa Notification Packages registry value, which enables password filter DLLs to intercept cleartext credentials during every password change operation. APT34 uses this technique to register malicious password filter DLLs for persistent credential harvesting. Legitimate changes to this value are extremely rare.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL
Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL

query: registry where event.action == "modification" and registry.path : "HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Lsa\\Notification Packages" and not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\lsass.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\services.exe")
note:
## Triage
- CRITICAL: This is a high-fidelity detection - legitimate modifications to Notification Packages are extremely rare
- Immediately identify the process that modified the registry value
- Check what DLL name was added to Notification Packages
- Verify the DLL exists on disk and inspect its exports (InitializeChangeNotify, PasswordChangeNotify, PasswordFilter)
- If unauthorized, treat as active credential compromise
- Check for credential dump files on disk

## False Positives
- Legitimate enterprise password filter software installation (very rare)
- Password policy enforcement tools (e.g., Specops, nFront)
false_positives: Legitimate enterprise password filter software installation (very rare)
Password policy enforcement tools (e.g., Specops, nFront)

name: Suspicious Password Filter DLL File Creation
description: Detects creation of DLL files with names matching password filter naming conventions (credfilter, passfilt, pwdfilter, password_filter). These DLLs may be intended for registration as LSA password filters to intercept cleartext credentials.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL

query: file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "dll" and file.name : ("*credfilter*", "*passfilt*", "*pwdfilter*", "*password_filter*", "*PasswordFilter*", "*authfilter*") and not file.name : ("rassfm.dll", "scecli.dll", "FVENOTIFY.dll", "kdcsvc.dll")
note:
## Triage
- Check the DLL exports for password filter interface functions
- Correlate with LSA Notification Packages registry modification
- Verify the source process and user account
- Inspect the DLL for credential logging or exfiltration capabilities

## False Positives
- Enterprise password policy enforcement tools
- Security assessment tools deploying test password filters
false_positives: Enterprise password policy enforcement tools
Security assessment tools deploying test password filters

name: PowerShell Exchange Web Services Interaction - Email C2 Indicator
description: Detects PowerShell processes interacting with Exchange Web Services (EWS) API endpoints, which may indicate email-based C2 like APT34's PowerExchange backdoor. PowerExchange monitors Exchange mailboxes for emails with @@ subject markers as C2 command delivery mechanism.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
query: process where event.action == "start" and process.name : ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe") and process.args : ("*Exchange.asmx*", "*New-WebServiceProxy*", "*ExchangeService*", "*Get-MailboxMessage*", "*Search-Mailbox*", "*FindItems*")
note:
## Triage
- Review the full PowerShell command line and any referenced script
- Check Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104) for script content
- Look for @@ markers in email subject searches
- Determine if this is part of authorized Exchange administration
- Check for accompanying email file (.eml) creation

## False Positives
- Legitimate Exchange administration scripts
- Authorized mailbox monitoring or migration solutions
- Exchange health monitoring scripts
false_positives: Legitimate Exchange administration scripts
Authorized mailbox monitoring or migration solutions
Exchange health monitoring scripts

name: PowerExchange C2 Script Deployment - Exchange Backdoor
description: Detects creation of PowerShell scripts with Exchange or EWS-related names that may indicate deployment of email-based C2 backdoors like APT34's PowerExchange.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
query: file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "ps1" and file.name : ("*PowerExchange*", "*Exchange*", "*EWS*", "*Mailbox*") and not file.path : ("C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\Exchange*", "C:\\ExchangeSetup*", "C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\Exchange Server\\*")
note:
## Triage
- Read the script content for C2 indicators (@@ markers, EWS API calls, Invoke-Expression)
- Check if the script was deployed by an authorized admin or deployment tool
- Correlate with process execution events
- Look for accompanying email artifacts

## False Positives
- Legitimate Exchange management scripts in non-standard locations
- Exchange migration or setup scripts
false_positives: Legitimate Exchange management scripts in non-standard locations
Exchange migration or setup scripts

name: Outbound SMTP Connection from Non-Mail Process - STEALHOOK Pattern
description: Detects outbound SMTP connections (ports 25, 587, 465) from processes that are not designated mail transport agents. APT34's STEALHOOK tool exfiltrates stolen data by sending emails with binary attachments through Exchange transport or direct SMTP connections.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol

query: network where event.action == "connection_attempted" and destination.port in (25, 587, 465) and not process.executable : ("C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\Exchange Server\\*\\EdgeTransport.exe", "C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\Exchange Server\\*\\MSExchangeTransport.exe", "C:\\Program Files\\Microsoft\\Office*\\outlook.exe", "C:\\Program Files (x86)\\Microsoft\\Office*\\outlook.exe", "C:\\Program Files\\Mozilla Thunderbird\\thunderbird.exe")
note:
## Triage
- Identify the process initiating the SMTP connection
- Determine the destination SMTP server
- Check if the host is a designated mail server or relay
- Look for preceding data staging or archive creation
- Examine email content if captured by DLP or mail gateway

## False Positives
- Legitimate SMTP notification services on application servers
- Application-level email sending libraries
- Monitoring tools that send alerts via email
false_positives: Legitimate SMTP notification services on application servers
Application-level email sending libraries
Monitoring tools that send alerts via email

name: Data Staging Archive Creation in Suspicious Directory
description: Detects creation of archive files (ZIP, 7z, RAR) in directories with staging, exfiltration, or payload-related naming patterns. APT34's STEALHOOK tool compresses stolen financial data, credentials, and communications before exfiltrating via email attachments.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
Archive Collected Data: Archive via Utility

query: file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension : ("zip", "7z", "rar", "gz", "tar") and (file.name : ("*payload*", "*exfil*", "*stealhook*", "*dump*", "*harvest*", "*stolen*") or file.path : ("*staging*", "*exfil*", "*collect*", "*temp\\*"))
note:
## Triage
- Examine the archive contents if possible
- Check the source process and user account
- Look for preceding data collection activity (file copies, database queries)
- Correlate with outbound SMTP or HTTP connections
- Check for sequential email generation following archive creation

## False Positives
- Legitimate backup operations with staging directories
- Software build systems creating archives
- Data migration tools
false_positives: Legitimate backup operations with staging directories
Software build systems creating archives
Data migration tools

name: Credential Dump File Creation - Password Filter Output
description: Detects creation of files with credential dump naming conventions, potentially indicating output from password filter DLLs, LSASS memory dumps, or other credential harvesting tools used by APT34.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL

query: file where event.action == "creation" and file.name : ("*credential*", "*cred_dump*", "*hash_dump*", "*secretsdump*", "*password_dump*", "*ntds_dump*", "*sam_dump*", "*lsass_dump*", "*captured_cred*") and file.extension : ("log", "txt", "csv", "dat", "dmp")
note:
## Triage
- Read the file content to determine if it contains actual credentials
- Identify the process that created the file
- Check for LSA Notification Packages registry modifications
- Correlate with password filter DLL installation
- If credentials are present, initiate password rotation immediately

## False Positives
- Security assessment tools generating test credential reports
- Penetration testing activities with proper authorization
false_positives: Security assessment tools generating test credential reports
Penetration testing activities with proper authorization

name: APT34 Exchange Weaponization Kill Chain - Multi-Stage Correlation
description: Detects the sequential APT34 Exchange weaponization pattern: IIS backdoor DLL creation followed by LSA Notification Packages modification, indicating a coordinated attack combining persistent web shell access with credential interception. This two-stage sequence is characteristic of APT34 Exchange server compromise operations.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Modify Authentication Process: Password Filter DLL
Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol

query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=2h
  [file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "dll" and (file.name : ("CacheHttp.dll", "HttpProxy.dll", "WebCache.dll") or file.path : "*\\inetsrv\\*")]
  [registry where event.action == "modification" and registry.path : "HKLM\\SYSTEM\\CurrentControlSet\\Control\\Lsa\\Notification Packages"]
note:
## Triage
- CRITICAL: This detection represents the APT34 Exchange weaponization kill chain
- IIS backdoor deployment followed by credential interception setup strongly indicates APT34 activity
- Immediately isolate the affected Exchange server
- Check for email-based C2 artifacts (emails with @@ subjects)
- Look for data exfiltration (archive creation, outbound SMTP)
- Begin full incident response procedures
- Rotate all credentials on the affected domain

## False Positives
- Extremely unlikely - this two-stage sequence combining IIS module creation with LSA modification is highly specific to APT34
false_positives: Extremely unlikely - requires correlated IIS backdoor deployment and LSA modification
